title: SEGMENT 9 — Reprogramming efficiency: the rare-event problem

lines.text: SEGMENT 9 — Reprogramming efficiency: the rare-event problem
I've been talking as if you put OSKM into a fibroblast and it becomes an iPSC.
That's true, on average, but it's important to understand the statistics, because the statistics tell you something deep about what reprogramming is actually doing.
When you transfect a million fibroblasts with the four factors, you don't get a million iPSCs three weeks later.
You get, depending on the method, somewhere between one in a thousand and one in ten thousand cells successfully completing reprogramming.
The rest either die, stay fibroblasts, or get partially reprogrammed but stuck in some intermediate state that isn't quite pluripotent and isn't quite fibroblast.
The successful conversion rate is, frankly, terrible.
Less than 1%, often far less.
Here's the analogy.
Reprogramming is like finding a four-leaf clover by stepping on a field of grass.
You walk into the field — you treat the cells with OSKM.
Most of the plants are three-leaf clovers — most of the cells stay fibroblasts.
But here and there, by some combination of luck and the right local conditions, you find a four-leaf.
Those are your iPSCs.
They are rare.
They are not designed for.
You're just searching the field with selection conditions that let you spot them.
Why is the efficiency so low?
Several reasons, and they tell you something about the biology.
First, chromatin is hard to remodel.
The fibroblast genome is locked down in a particular configuration.
Heterochromatin marks.
DNA methylation.
Specific histone modifications.
To erase all of that and recreate the open chromatin state of an ES cell, the OSKM factors have to physically dismantle a lot of repressive machinery.
That takes many cell divisions.
Many opportunities to fail.
Many places where the cell gets stuck partway and never completes the journey.
Second, the cell has to divide many times.
Cell division is when chromatin gets relaxed enough to be remodeled.
If a cell isn't actively dividing, the reprogramming factors can sit there indefinitely and nothing happens.
That's part of why c-Myc helps — it forces the cell cycle forward.
Cells that don't divide enough don't reprogram.
Third, stochastic gene expression.

Even when the factors are inside the cell, whether or not they bind to a specific target gene at a particular moment is a stochastic event — driven by molecular noise, by which other proteins happen to be around, by random thermal motion.

For a cell to fully reprogram, it has to thread a long series of these stochastic events successfully — get the right genes on at the right times, get the right genes off, navigate the chromatin state space, and arrive at the pluripotent attractor.
The fraction of cells that thread the whole needle is small.
Fourth — and this is the deepest reason — becoming pluripotent is, in some sense, a fundamentally improbable event.
The pluripotent state is one specific attractor among many possible cellular states.
Most random walks away from the fibroblast state lead to bad outcomes — apoptosis, senescence, partial reprogramming.
The walks that lead specifically to pluripotency are a thin subset.
Reprogramming efficiency reflects how thin that subset is.
What this means in practice is that iPSC generation is, by necessity, a selection process.
You start with many cells, you let the rare successful events happen, and you isolate the colonies that successfully reprogrammed.
Each colony is, in principle, the descendant of a single rare reprogrammed cell.
You pick those colonies, expand them, and call them iPSC lines.
Each line is, in effect, the lineage of one four-leaf clover that you happened to step on.
This has practical consequences.
It means iPSC lines from the same starting cells aren't necessarily identical.
Each one went through a different stochastic path through the reprogramming landscape.
They might differ in subtle ways — in their epigenetic state, in their differentiation propensities, in their behavior under stress.
Good iPSC practice involves making many lines from the same starting cells and characterizing each one carefully, so you know which line behaves how.
The low efficiency is also why partial reprogramming has become an interesting topic in its own right — which we'll come to in Segment 16.

If most cells, when exposed to OSKM, don't go all the way to pluripotency but do shift their state somewhat, can we use that partial shift for something useful, like reversing aging in adult tissues without actually making them pluripotent?
Spoiler: the answer is maybe yes.
